region_of_origin: Mediterranean (Laurus nobilis); widely used across South Asian, Southeast Asian, and European cuisines

ontology.foodex2_description: Bay leaf — dried herb/spice, Laurus nobilis

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving is 1–2 whole dried bay leaves (approximately 0.5–1 g) added to a pot of soup, stew, or curry and removed before eating.

nutrient_source.title: Bay leaf, dried — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macro values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for dried bay leaf (Laurus nobilis), confidence 0.95 as recorded in input metadata. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #02004 (Spices, bay leaf) which reports: energy 313 kcal, protein 7.61 g, fat 8.36 g, carbohydrate 74.97 g, dietary fibre 26.3 g, sodium 23 mg, potassium 529 mg, calcium 834 mg, iron 43.0 mg per 100 g — values are consistent. Extended micronutrient values (saturated fat, fatty acid breakdown, vitamins, additional minerals) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #02004.

gi_source.title: N/A — Bay leaf is a culinary herb used in negligible quantities; no GI value is established or applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Bay leaf is a dried herb/spice used in quantities of 0.5–1 g per serving. No glycemic index has been established for bay leaf in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is not applicable as the food is consumed in negligible culinary quantities and contributes no meaningful carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (23 mg/100 g) and high potassium (529 mg/100 g); actual culinary intake is 0.5–1 g per dish
hypertension.guidance: Bay leaf is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium and high potassium content are directionally beneficial, and the negligible culinary serving size means it contributes essentially no sodium to the diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (26.3 g/100 g) and bioactive compounds (eugenol, cinnamic acid) with evidence of improving insulin sensitivity; negligible glycaemic impact at culinary doses
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bay leaf is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; used in typical culinary quantities of 1–2 leaves per dish, it contributes negligible carbohydrate or glycaemic load, and its bioactive compounds have been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in preliminary research.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol at culinary serving sizes; high fibre and polyphenol content (eugenol, rutin, caffeic acid) with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bay leaf is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; it contains no cholesterol, negligible saturated fat per culinary serving, and its polyphenols (eugenol, rutin) have demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity relevant to cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (eugenol, linalool) and high calcium (834 mg/100 g) supporting bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bay leaf is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its eugenol and linalool content have documented anti-inflammatory properties, and its high calcium content supports bone health relevant to RA management — always remove leaves before serving to avoid choking hazard.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bay leaf (Laurus nobilis) is a dried aromatic herb widely used as a flavouring agent in cuisines across the Mediterranean, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and beyond. The leaves are dark green, oval, and firm, with a distinctive floral, slightly bitter, and mildly spicy aroma derived from volatile oils including eucalyptol (1,8-cineole), eugenol, and linalool. Bay leaves are almost universally used whole and removed from dishes before serving, as they are not palatable when eaten directly. They are added to soups, stews, curries, rice dishes, braises, and marinades to impart a subtle herbal depth. In Indian cooking, the dried bay leaf (tej patta) is a standard component of whole-spice tempering. In European cooking, it is a core element of bouquet garni. Nutritionally, dried bay leaf per 100 g is energy-dense (313 kcal) with significant carbohydrate (74.97 g), high dietary fibre (26.3 g), notable protein (7.61 g), and meaningful fat (8.36 g), but actual culinary intake per dish is only 0.5–1 g, making the per-100 g values largely academic. Bay leaf is exceptionally rich in calcium (834 mg/100 g) and iron (43 mg/100 g) and has a very favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (23 mg sodium vs 529 mg potassium per 100 g). Bioactive compounds including eugenol, cinnamic acid, and rutin have been studied for anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and insulin-sensitising properties. Bay leaf is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, with directionally positive nutritional and phytochemical properties at typical culinary doses.
common_user_queries: What is bay leaf used for in cooking?
Is bay leaf good for diabetes?
Is bay leaf the same as daun salam?
Bay leaf for high blood pressure
What does bay leaf taste like?
How many bay leaves should I use in a recipe?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #02004 (Spices, bay leaf) which reports identical values. Extended micronutrient values (saturated fat, vitamins, minerals) sourced from USDA SR Legacy #02004. Source match is exact for both primary and secondary sources.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA SR Legacy; bay leaf is a plant-derived herb and naturally contains no cholesterol. 2. Trans fat is 0.0 g — naturally zero for a minimally processed dried herb. 3. Sugar is 0.0 g — USDA SR Legacy reports 0.0 g sugar for dried bay leaf; confirmed zero. 4. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg and Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as bay leaf is a plant food with no animal-derived components. 5. Saturated fat (2.28 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.64 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.29 g), and all micronutrient values not present in the HPB input are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #02004 (Spices, bay leaf). 6. GI is not applicable: bay leaf is used in culinary quantities of 0.5–1 g per dish and contributes no meaningful carbohydrate load; no GI value is established in the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable. 7. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as it is not applicable to a dried herb/spice leaf. 8. Daun salam (Indonesian/Malay) technically refers to Syzygium polyanthum (Indonesian bay leaf), which is botanically distinct from Laurus nobilis (Mediterranean bay leaf); the alias is retained for retrieval purposes but users should be aware of this distinction. 9. Iron value of 43.0 mg/100 g is exceptionally high and reflects the concentrated dried form; actual iron contribution per culinary serving (0.5–1 g) is negligible. 10. Aliases consistency self-check (q8): all strings present in aliases_by_language values — Bay Leaf, Bay leaf, Bay laurel leaf, Laurel leaf, Sweet bay leaf, Mediterranean bay leaf, Dried bay leaf, Laurus nobilis, 月桂叶, 月桂葉, Daun salam, Daun bay, பே இலை, தேய்ப்பிலை, तेज पत्ता, Tej patta — verified present verbatim in flat aliases array. Previous iteration q8 failure was caused by 'तेज पत्ta' (corrupted encoding) in aliases_by_language hindi entry; corrected to 'तेज पत्ता' in this iteration and verified consistent.
